ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for coin prata; mapped to the closest parent class 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315595) as a defensible approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Flat bread) is the closest defensible category for coin prata, a fried flatbread. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this Singaporean-Indian preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for coin prata or roti prata in the clinical terminology system.

serving.description: 3–4 pieces of coin prata with a serving of fish curry
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of coin prata consists of 3–4 bite-sized pieces (approximately 350 g including curry), commonly eaten at breakfast or as a snack at Singaporean hawker centres and mamak stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Coin prata is a miniature, bite-sized variant of plain roti prata. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for roti prata, which is the canonical approved source for this dish. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Bread, Indian, roti, whole wheat; USDA FDC ID 174263) as the HPB entry does not report these micronutrients. The coin prata preparation is nutritionally equivalent to plain roti prata per 100 g; the smaller piece size affects portion weight only, not per-100 g composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coin prata specifically. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain roti / chapati made from refined wheat flour, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 Atkinson et al. international GI tables (GI range 57–70 for wheat-flour flatbreads; median 62 used). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 263.3 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.2. Carbohydrate per serving = 75.23 g/100 g × 350 g / 100 = 55.16 g × 0.62 = 34.2 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coin prata to 1–2 pieces per sitting, as a 350 g serving delivers approximately 2,212 mg sodium, well above the 1,500–2,000 mg daily target; request preparation with reduced ghee and avoid high-sodium curry accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (75.23 g/100 g) with medium GI (62) produces significant postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict coin prata to 1–2 small pieces per meal, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose post-meal, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 55 g of available carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~34.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g from ghee and trans fat 0.14 g/100 g, combined with high sodium 632 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 1–2 coin pieces and request preparation with minimal ghee, as the combination of saturated fat (5.14 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and high sodium (632 mg/100 g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g is borderline pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit coin prata to 1–2 pieces to reduce sodium-driven tissue oedema and request less ghee in preparation to lower saturated fat intake, which can upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coin prata is a bite-sized, crispy variant of the classic Singaporean-Indian roti prata flatbread. It is made from a simple dough of refined wheat flour, water, ghee (clarified butter), and salt, which is hand-stretched into thin layers, folded repeatedly to create a flaky laminated structure, and then pan-fried on a flat iron griddle (tawa) with ghee until golden and crisp on both sides. The resulting pieces are small, coin-shaped discs approximately 5–7 cm in diameter, with a shatteringly crisp exterior and a soft, layered interior. Coin prata is typically served in portions of 3–6 pieces alongside fish curry, dhal, or sugar, and is a popular breakfast and supper item at Singaporean hawker centres, mamak stalls, and Indian Muslim restaurants. Nutritionally, coin prata is energy-dense at 412 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 75.23 g/100 g from refined wheat flour, moderate protein at 9.85 g/100 g, and fat at 8.25 g/100 g largely from ghee. Sodium is elevated at 632 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension management. The saturated fat content of 5.14 g/100 g from ghee is relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk. Despite a dietary fibre content of 10.2 g/100 g, the high refined carbohydrate load and medium glycaemic index of approximately 62 make it unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by people with type 2 diabetes. Coin prata is halal-certified in most Singaporean establishments and is a culturally significant dish in the Singaporean Indian and Malay communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in coin prata?
What is the difference between coin prata and regular prata?
How much sodium in coin prata?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The coin prata is a well-documented variant of plain roti prata; per-100 g composition is nutritionally equivalent to the HPB roti prata entry. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the closest approved secondary source. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous flatbread entry with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for coin prata / roti prata is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy wheat flatbread entries as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo roti prata entry and are not available in the USDA SR Legacy closest analogue with sufficient confidence for this preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB Foodo for plain roti prata (egg-free preparation); confirmed zero per HPB source. Two portions are listed in the input; both are retained as contextual serving references from GrabFood, a secondary commercial source used for portion context only, not for nutrient values. The input listed 'primary_cuisine' as Malay; this has been corrected to Singaporean Indian as coin prata is an Indian-origin flatbread popularised in Singapore's Indian Muslim hawker culture, though it is widely consumed across all ethnic communities in Singapore.
